learningGoal: How to simplify a fraction by dividing top and bottom by the same number
How to find the highest common factor (the biggest number that divides into both) to get the simplest form

screens.title: Can you simplify 6/8?
screens.body: Ella is sharing sweets at a party. She used **6/8** of the sweets. But there's a **simpler** way to write that fraction!

screens.title: Divide top and bottom by the same number
screens.body:
Let's simplify **6/8**.
Find the **biggest number** that divides evenly into both 6 and 8 — that's the **highest common factor**.

steps.text: Find the biggest number that divides into both 6 and 8
steps.why: This is the highest common factor
Divide the bottom number by 2 too

screens.title: Simplify 8/12
screens.body:
The **highest common factor** of 8 and 12 is **4**.
Divide both top and bottom by 4. What do you get?

interaction.question: What is 8/12 in its simplest form?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Divide both 8 and 12 by 4 to get **2/3**.

screens.title: How to simplify any fraction
screens.body:
The **highest common factor** is the biggest number that divides evenly into both the top and bottom.
Divide them both by it — that gives you the **simplest form**.

steps.text: Find the biggest number that divides into both the top and bottom
steps.why: This is the highest common factor

steps.text: Divide the top number by it
Divide the bottom number by it too

learningGoal: How to avoid stopping too early when simplifying a fraction
When to watch out for not using the highest common factor (the biggest number that divides into both)

screens.title: Can you spot Tom's mistake?
screens.body: Tom tried to simplify **12/18** and got **6/9**. But that's not the simplest form! Can you see what went wrong?

screens.title: Not fully simplified!
screens.body: Tom only divided by 2 instead of the highest common factor (6). You can still divide **6** and **9** further! The biggest number that divides into both 12 and 18 is actually **6**.

steps.why: Tom only divided by 2 instead of the highest common factor (6)

steps.text: The biggest number that divides both is 6
steps.why: Use the biggest one, not just any factor

screens.title: What's the simplest form?
screens.body: What is **4/6** fully simplified?

interaction.question: What is 4/6 in its simplest form?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 4/6 = **2/3**. You divided both by 2!
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The biggest number that divides both is 2. So 4 ÷ 2 = 2 and 6 ÷ 2 = 3. Answer: **2/3**.

screens.title: Always simplify fully!
screens.body: The most common mistake is **stopping too soon**. Check: can you divide top and bottom by the same number again? If yes, keep going!

steps.text: Find the biggest number that divides into both top and bottom
steps.why: Don't just pick any factor — find the biggest!

steps.text: Divide top and bottom by that number
steps.why: One step to the simplest form
If yes, you haven't found the biggest factor yet

steps.text: If nothing divides both, you're done!

learningGoal: How to compare fractions by converting to the same bottom number (denominator)
Why you can only compare fractions when the pieces are the same size

screens.title: Which is bigger: 2/3 or 3/5?
screens.body: Ben ate pizza at a party. Is **2/3** more or less than **3/5**? When the bottom numbers are different, you can't just look at the numbers!

screens.title: Make the bottom numbers the same!
screens.body: Which is bigger: **2/3** or **3/5**? To compare, both fractions need the **same bottom number**. Find a number that both 3 and 5 go into. Tap to see!

steps.text: Find the smallest number both 3 and 5 go into
steps.why: This is called the lowest common denominator (bottom number)

steps.text: Convert 2/3: multiply top and bottom by 5
Convert 3/5: multiply top and bottom by 3

screens.title: Which is larger?
screens.body: Convert both fractions to the same bottom number. **3/4** and **2/3** — which is bigger?

interaction.question: Which is larger: 3/4 or 2/3?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **3/4** is bigger. Convert to 12ths: 9/12 vs 8/12 — the bigger top wins!
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Convert to 12ths: 9/12 vs 8/12. **3/4** is larger.

screens.title: How to compare any two fractions
screens.body: You can **only** compare fractions when the pieces are the **same size** — that means the bottom numbers must match. Convert first, then compare!

steps.text: Find the smallest number both bottom numbers go into
steps.why: This gives you matching bottom numbers

steps.text: Convert both fractions to that bottom number
steps.why: Multiply top and bottom by the same number

steps.text: Write your answer using the original fractions
steps.why: The matching bottom number was just for comparing!

learningGoal: How to avoid comparing fractions without making the bottom numbers match first
When to watch out for assuming bigger numbers mean bigger fractions

screens.title: Can you spot Sophie's mistake?
screens.body: Sophie says: "1/8 is bigger because 8 > 4". Is that correct reasoning?

screens.title: You must convert first!
screens.body:
Sophie thought a bigger denominator means a bigger fraction.
You can only compare when the bottom numbers are the **same**!

steps.why: thought a bigger denominator means a bigger fraction

steps.text: Make the bottom numbers the same: use 8

screens.title: Which is bigger: 3/5 or 1/2?
screens.body: Think carefully — which fraction represents more?

interaction.question: Which is bigger?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **3/5** is bigger. Convert to the same bottom number to prove it!
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not this time! Convert to the same bottom number and compare. **3/5** is the bigger fraction.

screens.title: Common comparing traps!
screens.body: Don't fall for these tricks! Always convert to the **same bottom number** before comparing.

steps.text: Bigger bottom number does NOT mean bigger fraction
steps.why: 1/8 is smaller than 1/4 — the pieces are tinier!

steps.text: You can't compare tops if the bottom numbers are different
Convert to the same bottom number, then compare tops

learningGoal: How to add fractions when the bottom numbers (denominators) are the same
Why we add the top numbers but keep the bottom number the same

screens.body: Daisy is eating slices of cake. First **2/8**, then another **3/8**. How much altogether?

screens.title: Add the tops, keep the bottom!
screens.body: When the bottom numbers are the **same**, the pieces are already the same size. Just **add the top numbers** and keep the bottom number!

steps.why: The piece size stays the same

screens.title: What is 2/9 + 3/9?
screens.body: Add the top numbers, keep the bottom number!

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 2 + 3 = 5, and the bottom number stays as 9. Answer: **5/9** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Add the tops: 2 + 3 = 5. Keep the bottom: 9. Answer: **5/9**.

screens.title: Adding fractions with the same bottom number
screens.body: This is the easiest type of fraction addition. Same bottom number = same-sized pieces, so just count how many pieces you have!

steps.text: Check: are the bottom numbers the same?
Keep the bottom number the same

steps.why: Check if top and bottom share a factor

learningGoal: How to apply same-bottom-number addition to real-life sharing problems
When to watch out for accidentally adding the bottom numbers too

screens.title: A common trap!
screens.body: Oscar eats 1/5 of a chocolate bar before lunch and 2/5 after lunch. Someone says the answer is **3/10**. Does that make sense? Think about how much of the chocolate bar that would be...

screens.title: Never add the bottom numbers!
screens.body: The wrong answer **3/10** came from adding tops AND bottoms. But the bottom number tells you the **size of the pieces** — it doesn't change when you add! Only the **top numbers** get added.

steps.why: added both tops AND bottoms — this is WRONG

interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 2 + 4 = 6, keep the bottom number 9. Answer: **6/9** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Remember: NEVER add the bottom numbers! 2 + 4 = 6, bottom number stays at 9. Answer: **6/9**.

screens.title: The golden rule of fraction addition!
screens.body: When adding fractions with the **same bottom number**: add the tops, keep the bottom. **Never** add the bottom numbers!

steps.text: Keep the bottom number the same
steps.why: The piece size is already the same!
This is the most common fraction mistake

learningGoal: How to subtract fractions when the bottom numbers (denominators) are the same
Why we subtract the top numbers but keep the bottom number the same

screens.body: Holly had a chocolate bar and gave some away. She started with **5/8** and used **2/8**. How much is left?

screens.title: Subtract the tops, keep the bottom!
screens.body: Just like addition — but we **subtract** the top numbers instead. The bottom number stays the same because the piece size hasn't changed!

steps.why: The piece size stays the same

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! 7 − 2 = 5, bottom number stays as 9. Answer: **5/9** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Subtract the tops: 7 − 2 = 5. Keep the bottom: 9. Answer: **5/9**.

screens.title: Subtracting fractions with the same bottom number
screens.body: Same rule as adding — just subtract instead. The bottom number **never changes** when you add or subtract fractions with the same bottom number.

steps.text: Check: are the bottom numbers the same?
steps.why: If yes, you're good to go!

steps.text: Keep the bottom number the same

steps.why: Check if a number divides evenly into both top and bottom

learningGoal: How to avoid subtracting the bottom numbers as well as the top numbers
When to watch out for changing the bottom number during subtraction

screens.title: Can you spot Finn's mistake?
screens.body: Finn tried to work out **5/6 − 2/6** and got **3/0**. Something went wrong!

screens.title: The bottom number stays the same!
screens.body: Finn subtracted BOTH the tops and the bottoms. The bottom number tells you the **piece size** — it doesn't change when you subtract. Only subtract the **top numbers**!

steps.why: subtracted BOTH the tops and the bottoms

screens.title: What is 7/9 − 4/9?
screens.body: Remember: only the top number changes!

interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 7 − 4 = 3, bottom number stays at 9. Answer: **3/9** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Only subtract the tops: 7 − 4 = 3. The bottom stays at 9. Answer: **3/9**.

screens.title: Never subtract the bottom numbers!
screens.body: The bottom number is the **size of each piece**. When you add or subtract fractions with the same bottom number, **only the top number changes**.

steps.text: Keep the bottom number exactly the same

steps.why: Check if a number divides evenly into both top and bottom

learningGoal: How to find a common bottom number (denominator) before adding
How to convert both fractions so the bottom numbers match

screens.body: Sophie is mixing paint for art class. She uses **1/3** of a tin and then **1/4** more. The bottom numbers are **different** — how do we add them?

screens.title: Make the bottom numbers match first!
screens.body: Let's work out **1/3 + 1/4**. You can't add fractions when the **denominator** (bottom number) is different — the pieces are different sizes! Find a **common denominator** (the same bottom number for both fractions) and convert both fractions.

steps.text: Find the smallest number both 3 and 4 go into
Convert 1/3: multiply top and bottom by 4
Convert 1/4: multiply top and bottom by 3

steps.why: Keep the bottom number the same
Can't simplify — no number divides evenly into both

screens.title: What is 2/3 + 1/5?
screens.body: Find the LCD (Lowest Common Denominator) of 3 and 5, convert both fractions, then add the top numbers!

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Convert to 15ths first, then add the tops. Answer: **13/15** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Convert both to 15ths: 10/15 + 3/15 = 13/15.

screens.title: Adding fractions with different bottom numbers
screens.body: Different bottom numbers = different-sized pieces. You **must** convert to the same bottom number first, then add the top numbers.

steps.text: Find the smallest number both bottom numbers go into
steps.why: This is called the lowest common denominator (the same bottom number for both fractions)

steps.text: Convert BOTH fractions to that bottom number
steps.why: Multiply top and bottom of each fraction
Now the pieces are the same size!
Check if a number divides evenly into both top and bottom ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid adding fractions with different bottom numbers without converting first
When to watch out for only converting one fraction instead of both

screens.title: Can you spot Jake's mistake?
screens.body: Jake says **1/2 + 1/4 = 2/6**. Something is very wrong here!

screens.title: You can't add the bottom numbers!
screens.body: Jake added both the numerators AND the denominators. This is the **number one fraction mistake**! You must find a common denominator (the same bottom number for both fractions) first. Remember: the denominator (the bottom number of a fraction) must match before you add!

steps.why: added both the numerators AND the denominators — WRONG!

steps.text: ✓ Step 1: smallest number both 2 and 4 go into = 4

screens.title: What is 1/3 + 1/4?
screens.body: Find the LCD (Lowest Common Denominator), convert, then add!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Remember: find the LCD (12), convert, then add the tops. 4 + 3 = 7. Answer: **7/12**.

screens.title: The biggest fraction trap!
screens.body: **Never add the bottom numbers.** This is the most common mistake in fractions. Always find a common bottom number first!

steps.why: You can't just add tops and bottoms!

steps.text: Match the bottom numbers → Convert → Add tops → Simplify
steps.why: This is the correct method every time ✓

learningGoal: How to find a common bottom number (denominator) before subtracting
Why 2/3 − 1/6 needs converting before you can subtract

screens.body: Priya had ribbon and cut some off. She started with **2/3** of a metre and used **1/6**. The bottom numbers are different — what do we do?

screens.title: Convert first, then subtract!
screens.body: Let's work out **2/3 − 1/6**. Just like adding — find a common denominator (the same bottom number for both fractions), convert both fractions, then subtract. The denominator (the bottom number of a fraction) must match first!

steps.text: Find the smallest number both 3 and 6 go into
Convert 2/3: multiply top and bottom by 2
Convert 1/6: multiply top and bottom by 1

steps.why: Keep the bottom number the same

screens.title: What is 3/4 − 1/5?
screens.body: Find the LCD (Lowest Common Denominator) of 4 and 5, convert both fractions, then subtract the top numbers!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Convert to 20ths, subtract the tops: 15 − 4 = 11. Answer: **11/20** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! LCD = 20. Convert: 15/20 − 4/20 = 11/20.

screens.title: Subtracting fractions with different bottom numbers
screens.body: Same method as adding — but subtract! Find the smallest number both bottom numbers go into, convert both fractions, then subtract the top numbers.

steps.text: Find the smallest number both bottom numbers go into
Convert both fractions to that bottom number
steps.why: Multiply top and bottom of each
The bottom numbers are now the same!

steps.text: Simplify your answer if you can
steps.why: Check if a number divides evenly into both top and bottom ✓

learningGoal: How to apply common-bottom-number subtraction to real problems
When to watch out for subtracting before the bottom numbers match

screens.title: Can you subtract these?
screens.body: Ravi has 3/4 of a pizza. He gives 1/3 to his sister. How much pizza is left? The tricky part: the bottom numbers are **different**!

screens.title: Same trick as adding!
screens.body: Convert both to the **same bottom number** (12), then subtract the tops. Tap to see!

steps.text: LCD (Lowest Common Denominator (the same bottom number for both fractions)) of 4 and 3 = 12

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Convert to 15ths first: 12 − 5 = 7. Answer: **7/15**.

screens.title: Adding or subtracting — same first step!
screens.body: Whether you're adding or subtracting fractions with different bottom numbers, the method is the same: **convert first**, then add or subtract the top numbers.

steps.text: Find the smallest number both bottom numbers go into
steps.why: Works for both adding and subtracting
Multiply top and bottom by the same number

steps.text: Add or subtract the top numbers
steps.why: Now the pieces are the same size

learningGoal: How to find a fraction of a number: divide by the bottom, multiply by the top
Why finding 1/4 first helps you find 3/4

screens.title: What is 3/4 of 40?
screens.body: Ella has a bag of marbles. She has **40 marbles** and wants to find **3/4** of them. There's a two-step trick!

screens.title: Divide by the bottom, multiply by the top!
screens.body: Divide by the **denominator** (bottom number) to find **one part**, then multiply by the **numerator** (top number). Tap to see!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **2/3 of 36**? Divide by 3, then multiply by 2!

interaction.question: What is 2/3 of 36?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Divide first: 36 ÷ 3 = 12. Then multiply: 12 × 2 = **24**.

screens.title: Finding a fraction of any amount
screens.body: Two steps — works every time! **Divide** by the bottom, **multiply** by the top.

steps.text: Divide the amount by the denominator (the bottom number)
Multiply by the numerator (the top number)
steps.why: This gives you the number of parts you need

learningGoal: How to apply the divide-then-multiply method to money and sharing problems
When to watch out for multiplying before dividing

screens.title: Which step comes first?
screens.body: Tom has 36 football stickers. He gives 2/3 to his friend. Someone got **72**. That's wrong! They multiplied 36 by 2 instead of dividing first. What should they have done first?

screens.title: Divide by the BOTTOM first!
screens.body: Always divide by the **denominator (the bottom number)** first. This gives you **one part**. Then multiply by the **numerator (the top number)** to get the right number of parts.

steps.why: multiplied 36 by 2 instead of dividing first

screens.title: What is 2/7 of 42?
screens.body: Remember: divide by 7 first, then multiply by 2!

interaction.question: 2/7 of 42 = ?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Divide first: 42 ÷ 7 = 6. Then multiply: 6 × 2 = **12**.

screens.title: The order matters!
screens.body: Divide by the **bottom** FIRST, then multiply by the **top**. Getting the order wrong is a very common mistake!

steps.text: Step 1: DIVIDE by the bottom number
Step 2: MULTIPLY by the top number
steps.why: Get the right number of parts

steps.text: Memory trick: 'Divide by the Down one'
steps.why: Down = Denominator (the bottom number of a fraction) = Divide!

learningGoal: How to turn a fraction into a decimal by dividing
How to recall which common fractions equal which decimals (1/2=0.5, 1/4=0.25, 3/4=0.75)

screens.title: What is 3/4 as a decimal?
screens.body: Aisha is measuring ingredients for baking and needs **3/4 kg**. The scales show decimals. How do we convert?

screens.title: Divide the top by the bottom!
screens.body:
A fraction **is** a division. **3/4** means **3 ÷ 4**.
Let's work through the division step by step. Tap to see!

steps.why: Put a decimal point after the 0
5 goes in the hundredths column — done!
These come up again and again!

screens.title: Convert 1/8 to a decimal
screens.body: What is 1 ÷ 8?

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 1 ÷ 8 = **0.125**. Remember: divide the top by the bottom.

screens.title: Fraction → Decimal — the recipe
screens.body: Converting fractions to decimals is simple: top ÷ bottom!

steps.text: 1. Divide the top number by the bottom number
2. Memorise the key pairs: ½ = 0.5, ¼ = 0.25, ¾ = 0.75
steps.why: These come up in nearly every paper

steps.text: 3. Check: is your decimal between 0 and 1?
steps.why: A proper fraction is always less than 1 ✓

learningGoal: How to apply fraction-to-decimal conversion in timed exam questions
When to use memorised equivalents instead of dividing every time

screens.title: Did you know that 3/8 = 0.375?
screens.body: Every fraction has a decimal twin! **3/8** is the same as **0.375**. The trick? Just divide: 3 ÷ 8 = 0.375.

screens.title: Fractions ARE divisions!
screens.body:
The fraction bar means "divided by". So **3/8** means **3 ÷ 8**.
Let's work through the division step by step. Tap to see!

steps.why: Put a decimal point after the 0
5 goes in the thousandths column — done!

interaction.question: 5/8 as a decimal = ?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 5 ÷ 8 = **0.625**. Divide the top by the bottom!

screens.title: The key fact!
screens.body: A fraction IS a division. To convert any fraction to a decimal: **divide the top by the bottom**. That's it!

steps.text: Divide top by bottom to get the decimal
Learn the common ones by heart!

learningGoal: How to turn a decimal like 0.6 into a fraction
How to simplify 6/10 to 3/5

screens.title: What is 0.6 as a fraction?
screens.body: Grace scored 0.6 in a test. Can you write **0.6** as a fraction? The decimal places give you a clue!

screens.title: Use the place value!
screens.body: Count the decimal places. One place = **tenths**, two places = **hundredths**. Then simplify!

steps.text: 0.6 has digits in the tenths column

screens.title: Convert 0.4 to a fraction
screens.body: Write 0.4 as a fraction in its simplest form.

interaction.question: 0.4 as a fraction = ?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 0.4 = 4/10. Simplify by dividing both by 2: **2/5**.

screens.title: Decimal to fraction in 3 steps
screens.body: Use the place value to write the fraction, then simplify. Works every time!

steps.text: Simplify by dividing top and bottom by the biggest number that goes into both

learningGoal: How to apply decimal-to-fraction conversion with different decimal lengths
When to watch out for forgetting to simplify after converting

screens.title: What do you notice?
screens.body: Look at this number line. **0.8** sits at exactly **4/5**. Can you see why?

screens.title: Decimals and fractions are the same thing!
screens.body: 0.8 means 8 out of 10. Write it as **8/10**, then simplify to **4/5**.

steps.why: Read the decimal as a fraction

screens.title: What is 0.2 as a fraction?
screens.body: Write 0.2 as a simplified fraction.

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 0.2 = 2/10, simplified = **1/5**.

screens.title: Decimals and fractions are two ways of saying the same thing!
screens.body: Count the decimal places to find the bottom number (10 or 100), then simplify. Easy!

steps.why: Divide top and bottom by the biggest number that goes into both ✓

learningGoal: How to turn a fraction into a percentage
Why percent means out of 100

screens.title: What is 3/5 as a percentage?
screens.body: Ravi scored 3/5 on a spelling test. What percentage is that? **Percent** means **out of 100**, so we need to convert 3/5 to something out of 100!

screens.title: Make the bottom number 100!
screens.body: Let's convert **3/5** to a percentage. Multiply top and bottom to make an **equivalent fraction** (a fraction with the same value but different numbers) where the bottom number becomes **100**. The top number then IS the percentage!

steps.why: Now we have the top number out of 100
These come up again and again!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 9/10: multiply both by 10 to get 90/100 = **90%**.

screens.title: Fraction → Percentage — the recipe
screens.body: To convert a fraction to a percentage: make the bottom number 100!

steps.text: 2. Multiply top and bottom by that number
steps.why: Equivalent fraction (a fraction with the same value) with bottom number 100

steps.text: 3. The top number IS the percentage

learningGoal: How to avoid dividing instead of multiplying when converting to a percentage
When to watch out for not converting the bottom number to 100 first

screens.title: Can you spot Marcus's mistake?
screens.body: Marcus says 3/5 = **35%**. That's wrong! Can you see what happened?

screens.title: You can't just write the digits!
screens.body: Marcus wrote the digits next to each other (3 and 5 = 35) instead of converting. To convert, you need to make the bottom number **100** (or multiply the fraction by 100).

steps.why: Just put the digits together — WRONG

screens.title: What is 2/5 as a percentage?
screens.body: Multiply by 100, not just write the digits!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 2/5 × 100 = **40%**. Always multiply the fraction by 100!

screens.title: Converting fractions to percentages
screens.body: Always **multiply by 100** or convert the bottom number to 100. Never just squash the digits together!

steps.text: Check: does your answer make sense?
steps.why: 3/5 is more than half, so it must be more than 50% ✓

learningGoal: How to turn a mixed number (a whole number and a fraction together) like 2 and 1/3 into an improper fraction (where the top number is bigger than the bottom)
How to turn an improper fraction (a fraction where the top number is bigger than the bottom) like 7/4 back into a mixed number (a whole number and a fraction together)

screens.title: 2 1/3 as one fraction?
screens.body: Charlie ate slices of pizza. That's **2 1/3** pizzas. Can we write this as a single fraction? Yes — it's called an **improper fraction (a fraction where the top number is bigger than the bottom)**!

screens.title: Multiply, add, keep!
screens.body: Let's convert **2 1/3** into a single fraction. Multiply the whole number by the **denominator (the bottom number)**, add the top, then keep the same bottom:

steps.why: Multiply the whole number (2) by the bottom number (3)
Add the top number (1) to get the new top

steps.text: Step 3: Keep the bottom number as 3

screens.title: Convert 3 2/5
screens.body: What is 3 2/5 as an improper fraction (a fraction where the top number is bigger than the bottom)?

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 3 × 5 = 15, then + 2 = 17. Answer: **17/5**.

screens.title: Mixed → Improper: the recipe
screens.body: Three steps every time. Let's use **2 and 3/4** as an example:

steps.why: Whole number × bottom number = pieces in the whole parts
Add the top number for the total pieces

learningGoal: How to avoid the common multiply-and-forget-to-add error with mixed numbers
When to watch out for writing the remainder (the amount left over after dividing) incorrectly as a mixed number (a whole number and a fraction together)

screens.title: Can you spot Sophie's mistake?
screens.body: Sophie tried to convert **9/4** to a mixed number (a whole number and a fraction together) and got **4 9/4**. What went wrong?

screens.title: Divide to convert!
screens.body: Sophie flipped it — put the denominator as the whole number. To convert an improper fraction (a fraction where the top number is bigger than the bottom): **divide** the top by the **denominator** (bottom number). The answer is the whole number, the remainder (the amount left over after dividing) is the new top number, the denominator stays the same!

steps.why: flipped it — put the denominator as the whole number

screens.title: Convert 11/3 to a mixed number (a whole number and a fraction together)
screens.body: Divide 11 by 3. What do you get?

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 11 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 2. Answer: **3 2/3**.

screens.title: Improper → Mixed: divide and keep!
screens.body: Divide the top by the bottom. The whole number is the answer, the remainder goes on top, and the bottom number **stays the same**.

steps.text: Denominator (the bottom number of a fraction) stays the same

learningGoal: How to multiply a fraction by a whole number
Why you only multiply the top number (numerator)

screens.body: Aisha eats 2/5 of a cake three times this week. That's **2/5 × 3**. How do we multiply a fraction by a whole number?

screens.title: Multiply the top, keep the bottom!
screens.body: When you multiply a fraction by a whole number, only the **numerator** (top number) changes. The **denominator** (bottom number) stays the same! If the answer is bigger than 1, convert it to a **mixed number** (a whole number and a fraction together).

steps.why: The piece size stays the same

screens.title: What is 3/7 × 2?
screens.body: Multiply the top by 2, keep the bottom the same!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly! Only multiply the TOP: 3 × 2 = 6. The bottom stays at 7. Answer: **6/7**.

screens.title: Multiplying fractions by whole numbers
screens.body: The most common mistake is multiplying the bottom number too. Remember: **only the top number changes**!

steps.text: Multiply the NUMERATOR (the top number of a fraction) by the whole number
steps.why: More pieces of the same size

steps.text: Keep the DENOMINATOR (the bottom number of a fraction) the same
If the top is bigger than the bottom, it's improper!
steps.why: Convert to a mixed number (a whole number and a fraction together) if needed
Multiply top, simplify if you can ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid multiplying both the top and bottom numbers by the whole number
When to watch out for forgetting to simplify the answer

screens.title: Can you spot Tom's mistake?
screens.body: Tom says **3/5 × 4 = 3/20**. The answer got *smaller*, not bigger! What went wrong?

screens.title: Multiply the TOP, not the bottom!
screens.body: We need to multiply the **numerator** (top number), not the denominator (bottom number). Tap to see!

bodyParts.content:
Tom multiplied the DENOMINATOR instead of the numerator. Multiplying the **denominator** (bottom number) makes the pieces smaller.
We need to multiply the **numerator** (top number) instead.

steps.why: Multiplied the bottom number instead of the top — WRONG
The bottom number stays the same

screens.title: What is 5/8 × 3?
screens.body: Multiply the TOP by 3!

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 5 × 3 = 15, bottom number stays 8. Answer: **15/8** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Multiply the TOP: 5 × 3 = 15. Keep the bottom: 8. Answer: **15/8**.

screens.title: Top goes up, bottom stays put!
screens.body: When multiplying a fraction by a whole number: **top number × whole number**, bottom number stays the same. If you multiply the bottom, you're making the pieces smaller — the opposite of what you want!

steps.text: Multiply the NUMERATOR (the top number of a fraction) by the whole number
Keep the DENOMINATOR (the bottom number of a fraction) the same
Check: is your answer bigger than the fraction?
steps.why: It should be, since you're multiplying!

steps.text: Simplify or convert to mixed number (a whole number and a fraction together) if needed

learningGoal: How to spot which type of calculation to use in a fractions word problem
How to check your answer makes sense

screens.title: What would you do?
screens.body: Lily has £60. She spends 2/5 on a book and 1/4 on lunch. How much does she spend altogether? This needs **more than one step**. Can you figure out the plan?

steps.text: Lily has £60. She spends 2/5 on a book and 1/4 on lunch.
steps.why: How much does she spend altogether?

screens.title: Break it into steps!
screens.body: The trick with multi-step problems is to **break them down**. Do one fraction at a time! Tap to see the steps.

screens.title: What's the answer?
screens.body: Tom has £80. He spends 1/4 on a book and 1/5 on lunch. How much does Tom spend altogether?

interaction.question: How much does Tom spend altogether?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Breaking it into steps: 1/4 of £80 = £20, then 1/5 of £80 = £16. Answer: **36 £** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 1/4 of £80 = £20. Then 1/5 of £80 = £16. Answer: **36 £**.

steps.text: Find each fraction of the amount, then add them

screens.title: Tackling fraction word problems
screens.body: Multi-step problems look scary, but they're just **small steps** chained together. Break it down and solve one bit at a time!

steps.text: Read carefully — what does the question ask?
Decide: what type of calculation do I need?
Work step by step — one calculation at a time
steps.why: Don't try to do it all at once!

steps.text: Check: does your answer make sense?

learningGoal: How to avoid picking the wrong calculation in a fractions word problem
When to watch out for answers that are bigger or smaller than they should be

screens.title: Did Jake get it right?
screens.body: A school has 120 pupils. 3/4 are girls. How many boys are there? Jake says the answer is **90**. Let's check their working...

screens.title: Check the reasoning!
screens.body: Jake found 3/4 of 120 but forgot to subtract — 90 is the number of GIRLS. Let's work through it properly step by step.

steps.text: ✗ Jake's mistake: found 3/4 of 120 but forgot to subtract — 90 is the number of GIRLS

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body: A class has 80 children. 3/5 are boys. How many girls are there?

interaction.question: How many girls are there?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Working step by step: 3/5 of 80 = 48 boys, then 80 − 48 = 32 girls. Answer: **32** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 3/5 of 80 = 48 boys. Then 80 − 48 = 32 girls. The correct answer is **32**.

screens.title: Avoiding word problem traps
screens.body: The most common mistakes: doing the right calculation but **forgetting the last step**, or **adding fractions with different bottom numbers wrongly**. Always re-read the question at the end!

steps.why: What exactly is it asking for?

steps.text: Re-read the question at the end
steps.why: Did you answer what they asked?
Too big? Too small? Makes no sense? Go back and check! ✓
